coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots from sour whey to develop a novel antimicrobial packaging for fiordilatte cheese
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Lacivita, Valentina
Tarantino, Francesca
Molaei, Rahim
Moradi, Mehran
Conte, Amalia
Alessandro Del Nobile, Matteo
coredata.dc:description: In this study, monodispersed and quasi-spherical C-Dots with an average size of 7.2 nm were successfully synthesized from sour whey solution by a hydrothermal method (200 °C for 9 h) for fiordilatte cheese packaging. C-Dots (2500 and 5000 mgL-1) were added to the cheese through an alginate-based coating or directly to the cheese brine. No significant changes in TM4 cell viability were observed at concentrations lower than 10,000 mgL-1. Microbiological and sensory properties of cheese coated and uncoated with C-Dots indicate a substantial preserving effect of the C-Dots. The uncoated control fiordilatte exhibited unacceptable levels of microbial proliferation within 3.5 days. Conversely, the coated cheese remained within acceptable limits, effectively doubling its shelf life compared to the control, primarily due to the coating protection rather than the addition of C-Dots. When compared to the control fiordilatte, the addition of C-Dots in the brine at 5000 mgL-1 resulted in an extension of over 10 days in cheese shelf life. Considering the significance of the sustainable approach in C-Dots synthesis and the exceptional use of C-Dots in the food industry, these findings hold great potential in terms of research and industrial applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fiordilatte
Shelf life
Sustainability
Cheese quality
Emerging technology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of C-Dots on fiordilatte microbiological quality
Effects of C-Dots on sensory quality

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support of Urmia University is appreciated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The images (A), UV–Vis absorption, fluorescence emission spectrum at excitation wavelength at 350 nm (B), particle size (C) and TEM micrograph (D) of C-Dots.
TM4 cells viability (%) during 24 h exposure to different concentrations of C-Dots using MTT assay. Dimethyl sulfoxide 5% was used as a positive control.
Microbiological acceptability limit (MAL), sensory acceptability limit (SAL), and shelf life (SL) values of fordilatte cheese.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: For each sample, the Shelf Life is the lowest value among its MAL and SAL values. Data are reported as mean ± SD. Values in each column with different superscript letters are significantly different (p < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Changes in yeast population in fordilatte during storage with different treatments.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Data are reported as mean ± SD. Values in each row with different superscript letters are significantly different (p < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The alterations in the number of mesophilic microorganisms in fordilatte during storage with different treatments.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Data are reported as mean ± SD. Values in each row with different superscript letters are significantly different (p < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The alterations in coliform population in fordilatte during storage with different treatments.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Data are reported as mean ± SD. Values in each row with different superscript letters are significantly different (p < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots from sour whey to develop a novel antimicrobial packaging for fiordilatte cheese

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Agricultural Sciences, Food and Environment, University of Foggia, Via Napoli, 25 - 71122 Foggia, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Agricultural Sciences, Food and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fanavaran NanoZist Plast Co., 1177 Urmia, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fanavaran NanoZist Plast Co., 1177 Urmia, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, 1177 Urmia, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, 1177 Urmia, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, monodispersed and quasi-spherical C-Dots with an average size of 7.2 nm were successfully synthesized from sour whey solution by a hydrothermal method (200 °C for 9 h) for fiordilatte cheese packaging. C-Dots (2500 and 5000 mgL-1) were added to the cheese through an alginate-based coating or directly to the cheese brine. No significant changes in TM4 cell viability were observed at concentrations lower than 10,000 mgL-1. Microbiological and sensory properties of cheese coated and uncoated with C-Dots indicate a substantial preserving effect of the C-Dots. The uncoated control fiordilatte exhibited unacceptable levels of microbial proliferation within 3.5 days. Conversely, the coated cheese remained within acceptable limits, effectively doubling its shelf life compared to the control, primarily due to the coating protection rather than the addition of C-Dots. When compared to the control fiordilatte, the addition of C-Dots in the brine at 5000 mgL-1 resulted in an extension of over 10 days in cheese shelf life. Considering the significance of the sustainable approach in C-Dots synthesis and the exceptional use of C-Dots in the food industry, these findings hold great potential in terms of research and industrial applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The fiordilatte is typically associated with southern Italy, widely appreciated for its great versatility, being consumed as a dairy product or as an ingredient in numerous food formulations. Fiordilatte is a soft spun paste cheese made from cow's milk and preserved in trays with brine at 4 °C (Conte et al., 2017). Being a fresh product with approximately 50–60% moisture content and a limited shelf life of 3–7 days, fiordilatte is considered highly susceptible to deterioration when exposed to thermal abuse. It is frequently compromised by various microbial agents, particularly Pseudomonas spp. and coliforms, that can lead to unpleasant odours and unappealing appearance (Lucera et al., 2014). A number of studies have been carried out to prolong the shelf life of fiordilatte, including modified atmosphere packaging (MAP), combined with active substances, such as potassium sorbate (Mastromatteo et al., 2015a) and lemon extract (Conte et al., 2007). Other studies have concentrated on the alginate-based coating containing active substances such as lysozyme (Conte et al., 2009), silver nanoparticles in combination with MAP (Matromatteo et al., 2015a), and silver nanoparticles embedded in the agar hydrogel (Incoronato et al., 2011). The results showed an extended shelf life of up to 10 days, due to the inhibition of microbial growth (Mastromatteo et al., 2015a). The silver nanoparticles embedded in the agar hydrogel exerted an inhibitory action on the Enterobacteriaceae, without influencing the sensory characteristics of cheese (Incoronato et al., 2011). Positive results in slowing down cheese spoilage were also demonstrated by dissolving essential oils of lemon, sage, and thyme in the brine (Gammariello et al., 2008). Furthermore, the potential use of non-thermal technologies (e.g., UV-C light) was also investigated on fiordilatte (Lacivita et al., 2016) which has resulted in reductions in Pseudomonas spp. without interfering with the colour and texture of the cheese. Another non-thermal technology used in fiordilatte was high-pressure processing. Evert-Arriagada et al. (2014) demonstrated that pressurized cheeses had a shelf-life of about 19–21 days if stored at 4 °C, while the control cheese became unacceptable within one week.

While certain non-thermal processes have produced interesting outcomes, the focus of research is shifting towards sustainable and cost-effective treatments that can deliver both effectiveness and simple applicability to address the limitations of potential industrial scalability (Orsato, 2006). In this context, carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon dots (C-Dots) could be a valuable alternative, also considering their scarce applications for fresh foods. C-Dots are nanostructures smaller than 10 nm and have attracted the interest of various research groups, due to their optical, electrical, and antimicrobial/antioxidant properties, but also for water solubility, low toxicity, and ease of synthesis (Dong et al., 2020). C-Dots can be amorphous or nanocrystalline and have sp2 carbon clusters, but several studies refer to a diamond-like structure consisting of sp3 carbons (Hu et al., 2009). There are two main synthesizing mechanisms for C-Dots, including top-down and bottom-up. According to Soni and Pamidimukkala (2018), C-Dots manufactured under heat treatment through the popular bottom-up process through ionization and polymerization, which is then succeeded by carbonization of low molecular weight organic precursors.

It is demonstrated that doped C-Dots represent additional and enhanced functionality over undoped C-Dots. For example, nitrogen-doped C-Dots exhibit an improved antimicrobial performance (Chatzimitakos & Stalikas, 2020). Normally, C-Dots are doped using chemicals such as N, S, and P, but as an alternative approach, researchers use biomass/by-products since these sources are inexpensive, available, and rich in carbon, nitrogen, sulfur, and other miscellaneous organic materials, leading to the production of heteroatom-doped C-Dots (Moradi et al., 2023). From the literature, the great variety of available wastes allows the production of C-Dots with different physicochemical characteristics, functional properties, and applications (Xu et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018; Zheng et al., 2015). Important sources of animal-based wastes/by-products that can be used for C-Dots synthesis include animal manure, blood, wool, poultry feathers, eggshell, and whey solution. Furthermore, it is interesting to note that it is possible to synthesize C-Dots using a simple hydrothermal treatment, starting from microorganisms and plant biomass/wastes (Choi et al., 2020; Kousheh et al., 2020; Meng et al., 2019). It is crucial to acknowledge that the cytotoxicity of nanomaterials is a significant concern for their application in food, and there are currently no well-established criteria for reporting cytotoxicity of different types of nanomaterials. This issue is further compounded in emerging nanomaterials such as C-Dots. C-Dots good biocompatibility ensures that they don't cause significant harm or pathological changes and can be excreted through normal metabolic pathways. C-Dots are safer for organisms than metal C-Dots. However, there is no established guideline and criteria for the use of C-Dots in food preservation (Ezati et al., 2022b; Zhao et al., 2022).

Due to well-known features including fillers and structural enhancement, antimicrobials, antifungal, antioxidants, and light blockers properties, C-Dots can be used as additives in packaging materials to provide a functional packaging material with an improved barrier and mechanical properties (Moradi et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2019). In this regard, incorporating C-Dots into cellulose films has increased mechanical strength (Cuevas et al., 2019), and when added to edible bio-nanocomposite films, C-Dots bring greater flexibility. C-Dots have also been applied to food to improve storability through direct addition (Zhao et al., 2020) and via active film and coating packaging (Ezati et al., 2022a) or food intelligent packaging (Ezati et al., 2023). While Fan et al., (2020) tested C-Dots on fresh-cut cucumber, Mei et al. (2022) recently assessed the effects of C-Dots on lychee, and Zhao et al., (2022) on banana pulp. These studies demonstrated the inhibition activity of C-Dots on different foodborne microorganisms.

The concept of C-Dots synthesis from dairy waste offers an efficient way to turn waste into biomaterials with antimicrobial performance. Given the presence of organic matter, i.e., nitrogen, and phosphorous, whey can be considered a suitable source for the preparation of heteroatom-doped C-Dots. This study aimed to examine the potential preservative activity of C-Dots, synthesized from sour whey solution using a hydrothermal technique, on fiordilatte cheese, given the recognized antimicrobial and antifungal properties of C-Dots (Rasheed et al., 2020); it is worth noting that there is no available information about C-Dots application on fresh dairy products. To evaluate the impact of C-Dots on the typical spoilage bacteria that thrive during cheese preservation, samples were exposed to a brine enriched with C-Dots and an alginate coating enriched with C-Dots, using different C-Dots concentrations to compare the results. To calculate the shelf-life, changes in pH and sensory features over time were also recorded during storage.

Sour whey (total solid 6.4%) was obtained from the Pegah Company of West Azerbaijan Province (Urmia, Iran), and employed for the fabrication of C-Dots by one-step hydrothermal carbonization. Two hundred milliliters of the solution were carefully transferred into a Teflon-coated stainless-steel chamber and incubated at 200 °C for 9 h. The resultant solution was centrifuged (15000 rpm for 20 min), filtered through a sterile PTFE syringe filter (0.22 µm), lyophilized, and refrigerated for further experiments. In order to confirm C-Dots synthesis, the size distribution, shape, and spectral properties, and spectrofluorometric scan of C-Dots were examined by a Scatteroscope particle size analyzer (PSA; Qudix Inc., South Korea), Leo 906 E for Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM; Carl Zeiss, Germany) and Thermo Scientific NanoDrop spectrophotometer (NanoDrop;2000C, Waltham, USA), JASCO fluorescence spectrophotometer (FP2200; South Korea), respectively. Moreover, to select an appropriate concentration of C-Dots with no or lower cytotoxicity for cheese application, the effects of different concentrations of synthesized C-Dots (310, 620, 1250, 2500, 5000, and 10000 mg.L-1) were evaluated on TM4 cells (mouse Sertoli cell line) during 24 h exposure time using the thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT) technique, according to the method described by Ghorbani et al. (2022).

The fiordilatte samples were purchased from “Posta la via” dairy company (Foggia, Italy) and brought to the laboratory under refrigerated conditions (4 °C). Each sample (50 g) was removed from its commercial packaging and placed in a sterile container, with a screw cap. Different samples were prepared: (1) the control fiordilatte in brine (CTRL); (2) the alginate-based coated fiordilatte in the brine (CTRL COAT); (3) the fiordilatte with the active alginate-based coating added with 2500 mg.L-1C- Dots, the coated fiordilatte was placed in the brine (COAT 2,5); (4) the fiordilatte with the active alginate-based coating added with 5000 mg.L-1C-Dots, the coated fiordilatte was placed in the brine (COAT 5); (5) the fiordilatte without coating, immersed in the brine containing 2500 mg.L-1 of C-Dots (LIQ. 2,5), and finally (6) the fiordilatte without coating, immersed in the brine containing 5000 mg.L-1 of C- Dots (LIQ. 5). The brine solution contained 6% NaCl dissolved in water. It was poured into each container in sufficient quantity (80 mL) to cover the entire cheese, regardless the coating and the C-Dots adoption. The selection of C-Dots concentration to be used was based on a preliminary toxicity assay.

To coat cheese, each sample was dipped in a sodium alginate solution (2% w/v) prepared by dissolving sodium alginic acid powder (MW 120.000–190.000 g/mol; viscosity 500–600 mPa*s; M/G ratio 1.8–2.2) (Farmalabor, Canosa di Puglia, Italy) in sterile distilled water, autoclaved at 121 °C for 15 min and tempered at 50 °C. For the coated cheese (samples 3 and 4), 1/3 of the water used to prepare the alginate was preliminarily used to dissolve C-Dots and then mixed with the coating solution. Calcium chloride (5% w/v) (Farmalabor, Canosa di Puglia, Italy) was used to promote the alginate gel formation process by immersing the fiordilatte in the solution for 1 min. To remove residual sodium chloride, the samples were rinsed with sterile distilled water. All the packaged samples were stored at 4 °C for 2 weeks. After being rinsed with sterile distilled water to eliminate any residual sodium chloride, all packaged samples were stored at 4 °C for two weeks. During this time, pH, microbiological and sensory quality were monitored.

In this assay, the microbiota/mycobiota of cheese samples were counted on microbial culture media purchased from Oxoid. Ten grams of cheese samples were diluted in 90 mL sterile saline solution (9 g/L NaCl) and mixed through a stomacher (Interscience, Saint Nom la Bretèche, France). Decimal dilutions were prepared in 0.1% peptone water. Total mesophiles were counted on gelatin pancreatic digest and incubated at 37 °C for 48 h, while violet-red bile agar and violet-red glucose agar were used to enumerate coliforms and Enterobacteriaceae at 37 °C after 24 h incubation, respectively. Pseudomonas spp. and yeast population were enumerated on pseudomonas agar base, using a selective CFC supplement and Sabouraud Dextrose Agar containing 0.1 g/L chloramphenicol, respectively, after incubation at 25 °C for 48 h. Finally, coco-shaped lactic acid bacteria and Lactobacilli numbers were counted on M−17 agar and de Man, Rogosa and Sharpe agar containing cycloheximide (0.1 gL-1; Sigma-Aldrich) after incubation at 37 °C for 48 h, respectively.
The pH determination was performed using a pH meter (Crison 2001, Crison, Barcelona, Spain). The measurements were carried out on both the homogenate samples and the preserving liquid.

The sensory quality of the cheese samples was assessed by 7 expert panelists, with many years of experience, over 15 days of storage. However, they were re-trained in 1 session/day for 2 days for a proper evaluation of cheese odor and texture using a 7-point scale (Chen et al., 2009), where a score of 4 indicates the limit at which the quality of the product was found to be unacceptable (Gammariello et al., 2009). For the analysis, the samples were presented simultaneously, and coated cheeses were served without any coating.

A modified version of the Gompertz equation was used to fit the microbiological and sensory experimental data (Conte et al., 2009). Specifically, the microbiological acceptability limit (MAL) was determined taking into account Pseudomonas spp. (106 CFU/g) and Enterobacteriaceae (105 CFU/g) as main spoilage groups of fiordilatte (Lacivita et al., 2016). The sensory acceptability limit (SAL) was calculated by using the sensory experimental data and setting the score equal to 4 as the threshold value (Lacivita et al., 2016). The MAL and SAL values were compared, and the lowest value among them represented the product shelf life.

Tests were carried out on duplicate batches and the experimental data are the average of two replicates. To determine whether significant differences (p < 0.05) existed among the fitting parameters, the one-way variance analysis (ANOVA) and Duncan’s multiple range test with the option of homogeneous groups were used through STATISTICA 7.1 for Windows (StatSoft, Inc., Tulsa, OK, USA).

The preliminary confirmation of the C-Dots synthesis was made visually and by spectrophotometric analysis. As shown in Fig. 1
A, the hydrothermal synthesis results in a brown solution (in daylight) that turns bright green upon UV excitation (220 nm). Several earlier reports have attributed green fluorescence to the C-Dots quantum confinement effect (Ghorbani et al., 2022; Salimi et al., 2021). The UV–Vis spectrum displays a net peak at 282 nm, assigning to the π-π* transition in the conjugated C = C and C = N functional groups (Fig. 1 B). Moreover, a tail extending observed at approximately 348 nm can be linked to the n − π* transition of the C = O and − NH2 groups (Zhao et al., 2019). The fluorescence emission results showed emission bands at 430 nm with a weak quasi-peak at around 465 nm (excitation wavelength of 350 nm). It is noteworthy that as the excitation wavelength gradually increased (290–400 nm), the emission peak positions shifted to a higher wavelength, and its intensity decreased dramatically after reaching the maximum (λemi: 430 nm, Fig. 1-B). This excitation-dependent peak may be related to the presence of carboxylic and amide groups or to a single transition mode reactor (Russo et al., 2019). According to the XPS results, the P2p peak is related to P-C, P-C/P-N, and P-O binding states, the C1s peak is attributed to the C-O/C-N, O-C = O bonding, the N1s peak is attributed to C = C-N, C-N, and N- H/pyrollic N, and O1s peak is assigned to C − OH/C − O − C, N-O, and C = O binding states in the synthesized C-Dots (unpublished data). In addition, the FTIR spectrum confirmed the existence of functional groups and characteristic bonds, including hydroxyl, amine, carbonyl, ether, etc. Notably, in the fingerprint region of the FTIR spectrum, absorption bonds assigned to the presence of P = O, P-O-C, P-O–H, P − O, Ca = O, and O-Ca-O groups were also observed (unpublished results). The synthesis of heteroatom-rich C-Dots was confirmed by comparing the obtained data with previous reports. The 1H NMR spectrum of C-Dots was obtained, which illustrated the signals linked to sp3 (C–H) protons, aromatic or sp2 protons, and strong signals in concordance with the aldehydic protons. In the 13C NMR spectrum, the hybridized carbon atoms corresponding to aliphatic (sp3) carbon atoms, the signals due to a carbon in the vicinity of an electronegative element (or carbon bounded to an electronegative element), and signals related to the C = C aromatic or sp2 carbons were identified (unpublished data). The PSA results confirm that 99% of the synthesized nanodots have dimensions lower than 17.1 nm, and the average particle size is 7.2 nm (Fig. 1 C). Furthermore, the TEM micrograph (Fig. 1 D) approved the uniform distribution, monodispersed, and quasi-spherical shape of C-Dots.

Toxicity is an important feature of C-Dots which is linked to C-Dots specifications and the type of cell line (Kumar & Kumar, 2021). In this study, C-Dots revealed a dramatic effect on the viability of TM4 cells only at 10,000 mgL-1, while no significant changes in cell viability were found at lower concentrations (310 to 5000 mgL-1) (Fig. 2 ). Consequently, C-Dots at 2500 and 5000 mgL-1 concentrations were chosen for inclusion in the alginate coating and the brine solution. Somewhat similar results were reported in the use of postbiotics-derived CDs at 1750 mgL-1 concentration in HCT-116 (Ghorbani et al., 2022) and 1000 mg.L-1 for citric acid-derived C-Dots in MCF-12A cell line (Esfandiari et al., 2019). There are no available toxicity criteria for C-Dots, but under a general criterion proposed by Huang and Tang (2021) for nanomaterials, the synthesized C-Dots can be considered to be potentially nontoxic nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of C-Dots on fiordilatte microbiological quality
ce:section.ce:para: This study examined the impact of C-Dots, at two distinct concentrations, on the microbial quality of fiordilatte. The C-Dots were incorporated as active compounds in either a sodium alginate coating or were dissolved directly in brine. The target microorganisms for evaluating the product microbial quality were Pseudomonas spp. and Enterobacteriaceae, as previous literature highlighted the role of these particular spoilage microflora in the deterioration of cheese quality in refrigerated storage (Lacivita et al., 2016; Mastromatteo et al., 2015b). Changes in the number of Pseudomonas spp. over time are reported in Fig. 3 . The figure shows that Pseudomonas spp. proliferated in all the samples during storage; however, there are different kinetics among the various tested cheese. In the control group (CTRL), the microbial concentration increased beyond the threshold (6 log CFU/g) after the 3rd day of storage. It took slightly longer than 6 days for COAT 2.5 and BRINE 2.5 samples to become unacceptable, whereas the COAT, COAT 5, and BRINE 5 required additional time to reach an unacceptable level of proliferation. In particular, the coating itself (COAT) and the highest concentration of C-Dots loaded in the coating (COAT 5) favored slower growth of Pseudomonas spp., which took longer than a week to achieve the undesirable microbial counts. When C-Dots at 5000 mgL-1 were dissolved directly in the brine (BRINE 5), the microbial inhibition was more pronounced, and the samples reached the threshold within 10 days. It’s worth noting that the worst sample was the CTRL, as it was microbiologically unacceptable in the few days following storage, therefore, the analysis of this sample was stopped after 10 days of storage. The best sample was the BRINE 5, most probably due to the abundant availability of C-Dots in contact with the cheese, compared to the corresponding coated sample (COAT 5). In fact, the C-Dots concentration in the coating was the same as in the brine; however, as the coating is much smaller than that of the brine, the amount of C-Dots in contact with the packaged fiordilatte is much higher in the case of BRINE 5 than COAT 5. It is worth mentioning that a portion of the C-Dots contained in the coating tends to diffuse into the brine, resulting in fewer C-Dots being in direct contact with the fiordilatte cheese.

There was also a significant inhibitory activity of C-Dots against Enterobacteriaceae (Fig. 4 ). As illustrated, also in this case the best result was obtained for BRINE 5 where the Enterobacteriaceae number never reached the threshold (5 log CFU/g) during the entire period of observation. The C-Dots directly dissolved in the brine exerted an inhibitory effect against this microbial group. In fact, the cell load reached a final concentration of about 4 log CFU/g. For the CTRL group, the Enterobacteriaceae rapidly increased from day 3 and passed the microbial acceptability limit after 6 days of storage (Fig. 4). For the other groups (COAT 2.5, COAT 5, and BRINE 2.5), it is possible to highlight a certain antimicrobial effect of C-Dots because in these samples the cell counts remained around 4 log CFU/g up to 10 days of storage, without significant differences (p > 0.05) between coating or brine and between the two C-Dots concentrations in the alginate coating. Data recorded on the above spoilage groups confirm literature data on C-Dots efficacy against pathogens and spoilers (Fan et al., 2020; Mei et al., 2022; Zhao et al. 2022).

The antimicrobial properties of C-Dots are connected to their adherence to the surface of bacteria, production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), disruption of bacterial cell walls/membranes, induction of oxidative stress, photodynamic activity, and damage to DNA/RNA (Romero et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2023). Our unpublished SEM observations demonstrate that whey-derived C-Dots induced changes in the morphology and integrity of cell membranes and ROS production. Gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria exhibited a completely disrupted and rough cell surface when exposed to C-Dots, but S. Typhimurium showed a greater degree of deformation. Notably, N-doping, enhances the antimicrobial effectiveness of C-Dots (Chatzimitakos & Stalikas, 2020). X-ray photoelectron spectrometer analysis revealed the presence of N and P in the structure of current C-Dots (unpublished data), which are linked to improved antimicrobial performance. However, the relationship between C-Dots and bacterial morphology is crucial, especially in the case of S. aureus clusters, where bacteria residing inside the cluster may provide defense against ROS damage caused by C-Dots. Interactions and entry of C-Dots into these clusters can be challenging (Nie et al., 2020).

To obtain more quantifiable information about C-Dots efficacy on the two aforementioned microbial groups in fiordilatte cheese, the experimental data were fitted by a modified version of the Gompertz equation (Lacivita et al., 2016). Table 1 reports the microbiological acceptability limit (MAL) calculated for Pseudomonas spp. and Enterobacteriaceae, indicating the number of days the product remained acceptable from the microbiological point of view. Data point out that the C-Dots enhanced the fiordilatte microbial stability by slowing the proliferation of both spoilage groups. The greatest antimicrobial efficacy was found for Enterobacteriaceae, which appeared to be more susceptible to C-Dots. The best result was obtained for the fiordilatte with C-Dots in the brine at 5000 mg.L-1 (BRINE 5), demonstrating that the direct addition of active compounds to brine is more effective than the addition to the coating.

Throughout the storage period, the cheese samples maintained highly stable pH values around 6 (data not shown). On the other hand, a slight decrease in pH was noticed in all the brines over time. It is unsurprising that the contamination of brine by microbial and fungal species led to a pH reduction during storage, as stated by Lacivita et al. (2016). This reduction was most noticeable in the brine of the control fiordilatte (from 6.45 to 5.25), due to the high microbial and fungal populations in this sample, compared to proliferation observed in the other active coated and uncoated cheese samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of C-Dots on sensory quality
ce:section.ce:para:
The sensory quality of fresh fiordilatte was evaluated in terms of odor and texture. It’s worth noting that after the addition of C-Dots into the brine, these nanomaterials moved the color from the usual pearly white cheese to a darker color, but this variation was not considered a sensory defect because the color resembled the typical dark tint of smoked mozzarella. Data presented in Fig. 5
, highlight the trends in cheese odor. The control became unacceptable after about one week. The coating seems to protect the quality of the cheese better than the active compounds directly added to the brine. However, all the cheeses with and without coating were acceptable throughout the storage period indicating that coating alone or the addition of C-Dots are effective to control the detrimental phenomena responsible for odor change. With high probability, microbial and fungal inhibition also favored sensory preservation (Lacivita et al., 2016). Very similar behaviors were recorded for the texture (data not shown). To calculate the number of days during that the products remained acceptable from the sensory point of view, the SAL values were also determined. Table 1 also gathers these parameters obtained from the fitting procedure. The sensory quality columns show that SAL was calculated only when the threshold score of 4 was achieved. For all other cases, the threshold was never reached, resulting in a SAL greater than the monitoring time, being reported in the table for most cases.

Shelf life was calculated for each fiordilatte cheese sample, following an approach outlined in the literature for this fresh dairy cheese. The shelf life was intended as the lowest value among all the MAL and SAL values (Lacivita et al, 2016). By examining the data listed in Table 1, it can be inferred that the Pseudomonas spp. proliferation primarily affected product shelf life. On the other hand, the growth of Enterobacteriaceae and sensory decay did not have a significant impact on cheese durability. Therefore, shelf life data coincide with MAL values of Pseudomonas spp. By comparing shelf life values among samples, some striking features can be underlined. As can be seen, control cheese became unacceptable within 3.5 days; the samples with coating remained acceptable for the double time (6–7 days), without any statistically significant differences among coated cheese (p < 0.05). Earlier studies have evaluated the ability of an alginate-based coating to preserve the quality of fiordilatte cheese. The results of the present study, which show an extension in the shelf life of the coated sample, are consistent with the outcomes reported in other investigations mentioned in the literature (Conte et al., 2009; Mastromatteo et al., 2015a). Results from Table 1 highlight that C-Dots incorporation into the coating did not prove to be highly effective in preserving the quality of the product. Similarly, the addition of potassium sorbate to the coating resulted in only a minor improvement in cheese preservation (Mastromatteo et al. 2015b).

On the contrary, the direct addition of C-Dots to brine at the highest concentration (BRINE 5) extended shelf life up to 10 days, suggesting that among tested solutions, BRINE 5 is the best treatment for maintaining the quality of fiordilatte cheese during refrigerated storage. Compared to other preserving strategies, the adoption of C-Dots in the brine represents a very promising technique. For example, numerous essential oils were tested in cheese brine but only a few of them were found moderate effective compounds to preserve cheese quality (Gammariello et al., 2008). Furthermore, the use of certain additives was often limited by their sensory impact, thereby restricting their application. Interesting results on fiodilatte shelf life were also recorded when the cheese brine was put in contact with an agar hydrogel loaded with silver nanoparticles (Incoronato et al., 2011). The extended and consistent release of silver biocide at a concentration level that was effective in providing antimicrobial efficacy was crucial for the design of these materials. However, the current safety regulations that limit the amount of silver ions that can be present in food matrices remain a significant obstacle to the widespread use of silver nanoparticles in the food industry. Another promising approach was recorded with the use of active films that were prepared by embedding copper nanoparticles via picosecond-pulsed laser ablation (Conte et al., 2013). While these active polymeric matrices were found to be effective in combating spoilage in fiordilatte, the shelf life prolongation was not as high as that promoted by C-dots dissolved in the brine.

As part of this work, C-Dots were synthesized via the utilization of whey, a substance that is commonly disregarded or not fully utilized in various countries, to enhence the shelf life of fiordilatte cheese. Alginate coating and cheese brine were utilized for the incorporation of C-Dots. During the storage period, C-Dots exhibited profound effectiveness in inhibiting microbial and fungal species, which proliferated in the uncoated cheese control, when specifically added to the brine at its highest concentration (5000 mgL-1). Comparatively, C-Dots displayed enhanced efficiency against Enterobacteriaceae than Pseudomonas spp. Therefore, the fiordilatte shelf life from 3.5 days in the control was doubled in the samples with alginate coating and reached 10 days in the samples with C-Dots dissolved in brine at 5000 mgL-1. The outcomes of the research have the potential to captivate the industrial sector for various reasons. Specifically, the dairy industry demonstrates a keen interest in prolonging the shelf life of fresh cheese. Moreover, effective and sustainable methods provide an appealing substitute for current systems to enhance cheese quality. Notably, the proposed solution is highly promising; it is also easy to implement in an industrial context, and it advocates a sustainable approach since sour whey serves as a foundational substrate for C-Dots synthesis. This approach enables the recovery of a by-product that is conventionally perceived as waste in the dairy industry.

Valentina Lacivita: Formal analysis. Francesca Tarantino: Formal analysis. Rahim Molaei: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Mehran Moradi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Amalia Conte: Conceptualization, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Matteo Alessandro Del Nobile: Conceptualization, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support of Urmia University is appreciated.
